system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 20: Rock Type Progressions

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 20 — Rock Type Progressions

_Pages 105-110._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Lowering 3rd and b7th yields IV9

> if you take any 7th chord and lower the 3rd and 7th 1 fret each, you will
> get a 9th chord built on the IV.

**Significance (p. 106).** States the dominant→IV9 voice-leading transformation rule.

## 3rd-b7th lowering general rule

> You actually can take the 3rd and 57th of ANY dominant chord, and by lowering them | fret each,
> you will get some form of a IV dominant chord

**Significance (p. 106).** Generalizes the lowering transformation to any dominant.

## Progressive lowering through cycle

> In any progression that has a series of dominant chords based on the cycle, you may progressively
> lower the 3rd and 57th, one fret at a time.

**Significance (p. 106).** Voice-leading rule through cycle-based dominant series.

## 3rd+b7 + melody as fragment

> you may just use the 3rd and b7th of any dominant chord and play melodies on top for an
> interesting effect.

**Significance (p. 106).** Defines the 3rd-b7-shell-voicing technique.

## Resolving diminished construction

> For an interesting sounding resolution of V7 to I, you may try putting together any 3 notes of a
> diminished 7th chord whose root is one fret higher than the root of the V7 chord, with the root
> of the I chord.

**Significance (p. 106).** Constructs the resolving diminished chord for V-I.

## Resolving Diminished defined

> This chord will now replace the V7 chord, and will be called a RESOLVING
> DIMINISHED CHORD.

**Significance (p. 106).** Names the technique central to Section 19.

## Resolving dim for m(maj7)

> Resolving diminished chords
>
> also work well in place of m# type of chords, as long as the 7th tone of the m+is in the resolving dim.
>
> chord.

**Significance (p. 107).** Extends resolving-dim use to m(maj7) substitution.

## Dominant 7 for major in rock

> for a rock sound, quite often for a
> major chord you can substitute the dominant 7th and its extensions

**Significance (p. 108).** Stylistic substitution rule for rock.

## I-IV-I sandwich

> each measure the I'V chord of each basic chord is sandwiched between
> two I chords.

**Significance (p. 109).** Names the I-IV-I sandwich pattern in rock.

## IV-of-IV insertion

> you may put in the IV of the IV before the IV.

**Significance (p. 109).** Extends the sandwich principle by cycle-stepping.

## Clockwise cycle motion

> When you do this, you are actually using the cycle in a clockwise motion.

**Significance (p. 109).** Identifies the IV-stacking technique as clockwise cycle motion.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
